skill.name: airflow-dag-patterns
skill.description: Build production Apache Airflow DAGs with best practices for operators, sensors, testing, and deployment. Use when creating data pipelines, orchestrating workflows, or scheduling batch jobs.
skill.summary: Create production-ready Apache Airflow DAGs using tested workflow orchestration patterns.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: The static findings are false positives caused by Markdown code examples, Airflow DAG syntax, and documentation links. No prompt injection, hidden execution path, credential access, data exfiltration, or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Python globals() Finding
dangerous_patterns.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Line 227 assigns generated DAG objects into the module namespace for Airflow discovery. The configuration source is hardcoded in the example and no untrusted input is executed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present, but the semantic context is a documented Airflow dynamic DAG pattern. I found no evidence of user-controlled code execution or malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Shell Backtick Findings
dangerous_patterns.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged locations are Markdown code fences or inline Markdown formatting, not Ruby or shell command execution. The skill is documentation-only and does not define runnable scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Every listed location is Markdown syntax or Airflow example text. There is no shell interpreter invocation, subprocess call, or install-time command.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Network Findings
dangerous_patterns.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Line 332 is an illustrative Airflow sensor calling a placeholder API health endpoint, and lines 521-523 are resource links. No hidden outbound request is made by the skill itself.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network call is visible sample DAG logic using example.com, and the remaining locations are documentation URLs. I found no evidence of credential or data exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Weak Cryptography Findings
dangerous_patterns.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines contain descriptive Airflow documentation text or links, not cryptographic API usage or weak hashing algorithms.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the listed lines showed no cryptographic algorithm, hash function, or secret handling. The blocker labels appear to be keyword false matches.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Reconnaissance Findings
dangerous_patterns.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines are Airflow DAG identifier creation, task dependency assertions, and a best-practice note about global state. They do not inspect the host or network.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines only manipulate Airflow metadata in examples. I found no calls that enumerate files, processes, interfaces, ports, hosts, or services.

content.value_statement: Data teams often lose time turning pipeline ideas into reliable Airflow workflows. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical DAG patterns for orchestration, testing, sensors, and error handling.
actual_capabilities: Explains core Airflow DAG design principles for idempotent, atomic, incremental, and observable workflows.
Provides example structures for TaskFlow API pipelines, dynamic DAG generation, branching, and conditional paths.
Shows patterns for S3 sensors, file sensors, external task sensors, and custom API sensors.
Covers failure callbacks, trigger rules, retries, cleanup tasks, and success notifications.
Includes pytest and DagBag testing patterns for DAG loading, dependencies, cycles, and task logic.
Outlines a production project structure for DAGs, plugins, tests, and shared modules.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not an installable Airflow package or DAG generator.
Examples use placeholder buckets, APIs, dates, and connection IDs that must be adapted.
It assumes basic Python knowledge and familiarity with Apache Airflow concepts.
It does not provision Airflow infrastructure, cloud permissions, or secret management.

use_cases.title: Design a new ETL DAG
use_cases.description: Turn a pipeline outline into a clear Airflow DAG structure with tasks, dependencies, retries, and scheduling choices.

use_cases.title: Review orchestration reliability
use_cases.description: Check an existing DAG design for retry behavior, failure handling, sensors, trigger rules, and operational observability.

use_cases.title: Add DAG tests before deployment
use_cases.description: Create focused tests that verify DAG import health, task counts, schedules, dependencies, and cycle checks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic DAG
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airflow-dag-patterns skill to design a simple daily ETL DAG. Include the task sequence, retry settings, schedule, and the key Airflow operators.

prompt_templates.title: Convert to TaskFlow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airflow-dag-patterns skill to convert my PythonOperator-based ETL workflow into a TaskFlow API design. Explain the task boundaries and XCom data flow.

prompt_templates.title: Add sensors and branching
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airflow-dag-patterns skill to add external dependency checks and quality-based branching to this DAG design. Include timeout and reschedule recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a production DAG
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airflow-dag-patterns skill to review this production DAG plan for failure handling, testing, observability, idempotency, and deployment readiness.

output_examples.input: Design a daily customer ETL pipeline in Airflow.
output_examples.output: A concise DAG plan with extract, transform, load, and notification tasks; daily scheduling; retry policy; dependency order; and testing recommendations.

output_examples.input: Review my DAG for production risks.
output_examples.output: A prioritized review covering non-idempotent tasks, missing timeouts, sensor worker usage, weak failure callbacks, and gaps in DAG import tests.

output_examples.input: Add an upstream file dependency and quality gate.
output_examples.output: A workflow outline using a rescheduled sensor, a data quality task, branch paths for quality results, and a join step with an appropriate trigger rule.

best_practices: Keep DAG files lightweight and move heavy business logic into importable modules.
Use retries, timeouts, callbacks, and trigger rules deliberately for each task.
Test DAG loading, task dependencies, schedules, and task logic before deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not put long-running computation directly in the DAG definition file.
Do not use blocking sensors when reschedule mode can release worker capacity.
Do not rely on global mutable state or non-idempotent task behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill create runnable Airflow projects?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and example structures that a user adapts into their own Airflow repository.

faq.question: Which Airflow version does it target?
faq.answer: The examples focus on Airflow 2.x patterns, including the TaskFlow API and modern sensor usage.

faq.question: Can it help with dynamic DAG generation?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains a factory-style pattern for generating similar DAGs from predefined configuration.

faq.question: Does it include testing guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers DagBag tests, task dependency checks, cycle checks, and direct task function tests.

faq.question: Does it configure cloud services or credentials?
faq.answer: No. The examples reference services such as S3, but users must configure their own connections and permissions.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production workflows?
faq.answer: Yes, as guidance. Users still need environment-specific review for security, scaling, monitoring, and deployment.
